passages.explanation: When the Sabbath is over, Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James, and Salome buy spices so they can go to anoint Jesus' body. Very early on the first day of the week, just after sunrise, they're on their way to the tomb. They ask each other: who will roll the stone away from the entrance for us? But when they look up, they see that the stone, which is very large, has been rolled away. As they enter the tomb, they see a young man dressed in a white robe sitting on the right side, and they're alarmed. He says: don't be alarmed. You're looking for Jesus the Nazarene, who was crucified. He has risen! He's not here. See the place where they laid him. But go, tell his disciples and Peter that he's going ahead of you into Galilee. There you will see him, just as he told you. Trembling and bewildered, the women go out and flee from the tomb. They say nothing to anyone, because they're afraid. The earliest and most reliable manuscripts of Mark end here — at verse 8, with the women silent and afraid. It's an abrupt, unsettling ending. The tomb is empty. The message is clear: he has risen, go to Galilee. But the women flee in fear. 'And Peter' is a detail of grace — the man who denied Jesus three times is specifically named and invited back. Mark's ending throws the story forward: the reader must decide what to do with the empty tomb, because the first witnesses haven't told anyone yet.

These verses are known as the 'longer ending' of Mark and are not found in the earliest and most reliable manuscripts. Most scholars believe they were added later to provide a more complete conclusion. They summarise Jesus appearing first to Mary Magdalene (from whom he'd driven seven demons), then in a different form to two disciples walking in the country, and finally to the Eleven while they're eating. He rebukes them for their lack of faith and stubborn refusal to believe those who'd seen him alive. He tells them: go into all the world and preach the gospel to all creation. Whoever believes and is baptised will be saved; whoever does not believe will be condemned. Signs will accompany those who believe: they'll drive out demons, speak in new tongues, pick up snakes, drink deadly poison without harm, and place their hands on sick people who will get well. After speaking to them, the Lord Jesus is taken up into heaven and sits at the right hand of God. The disciples go out and preach everywhere, and the Lord works with them, confirming his word by the signs that accompany it. Whether or not Mark wrote these verses, the original ending at verse 8 is itself a powerful literary and theological choice. The Gospel that began with 'the beginning of the good news' ends without a neat resolution — because the good news isn't over. It continues wherever the reader picks up where the frightened women left off.
